does: This paper develops a framework for predicting jailbreak vulnerability and transferring defenses across large populations of language models without evaluating each one exhaustively. The authors formalize the behavioral geometry of models by analyzing their responses to jailbreak attacks and use this geometric structure to predict which models are susceptible to attacks and which defenses transfer effectively. They evaluate their approach on 79 models from 24 providers and 100 configurations of a single base model, achieving 0.94 AUPRC for detecting susceptible models while reducing the number of required attack probes by approximately 98% compared to full per-model evaluation. Their method also outperforms naive provider-based defense transfer by 2% while maintaining this computational efficiency.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature on jailbreak attacks and red-teaming of language models, which has established that guardrails are vulnerable to adversarial prompts that bypass safety training. It extends prior work on defense transfer and model alignment by introducing geometric structure to understand and predict vulnerability across model populations. The paper connects to broader research on robustness evaluation and the fundamental challenge of efficiently assessing safety properties when the space of deployable configurations is exponentially large. It also relates to work on geometric properties of neural networks and feature-based model comparison, adapting these insights to the specific context of safety-critical evaluation where full testing is infeasible.
enables: This framework enables more efficient safety certification and deployment of large numbers of model variants without prohibitive evaluation costs. The behavioral geometry approach opens pathways for understanding the structural sources of jailbreak vulnerability across model families and training procedures, potentially informing better defense mechanisms at the training stage. Future work could extend this to predict vulnerability to novel attack types, develop more sophisticated geometric models that capture fine-grained behavioral differences, and use geometric insights to design models with intrinsically lower jailbreak susceptibility. The work also suggests possibilities for automated safety policy transfer and establishing behavioral similarity metrics that could improve model selection and configuration for deployment scenarios prioritizing robustness.
